title: The Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo
summary: The repository XML is a local copy of University of Michigan CME item DunTwa, a 1993 digital encoding of James Kinsley's 1979 edition, pages 42-58. Rickly describes Kinsley's complete text as based primarily on the sole surviving early print, which lacks lines 1-103, with variants from both the print and the Maitland Folio; the immediate source from which Kinsley supplied the opening has not yet been established. A complete 220-image published digital sequence of the NLS bound volume is publicly downloadable and cached, with this work mapped to images 177-189; image 189 is shared with the following work. No public digital facsimile of Pepys MS 2553 was found.
review_notes: The named Chepman and Myllar Prints volume is a bound collection. NLS classifies this tract as an additional item not printed by Chepman and Myllar, so collection context is not treated as printer attribution.

The Maitland witness runs from the poem's opening to its explicit but contains internal omissions relative to the print; the sole surviving early-print exemplar lacks its first two leaves and begins at poem line 104. Source relations therefore carry explicit line-level scopes and do not call either witness textually complete.

The public Internet Archive file whose metadata names Kinsley's 1979 edition is actually, on visual inspection, David Laing's 1834 volume 1. It is retained only as evidence for the Laing collateral edition, never as a Kinsley surrogate.
The XML profile's creation date of 1508 is inherited descriptive metadata, not proof of an exact composition date.
Access and rights statements apply only to the named entity and component; modern editorial layers, manuscript photography, and public-domain source texts are kept separate.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for DunTwa
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The file is 39,009 bytes at the pinned CME commit.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item DunTwa
entities.description: A keyed and structurally marked-up 1993 digital text of Kinsley's edition, retaining 530 verse lines and printed page breaks 42-58 while omitting printed front and back matter and editorial notes.

entities.label: The Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo (abstract work)
alternate_labels: The Tretis of the Twa Mariit Women and the Wedo
The Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo
entities.description: The abstract 530-line Older Scots poem by William Dunbar, distinguished from any particular witness, edition, or encoding.

entities.label: James Kinsley, The Poems of William Dunbar (1979), pages 42-58
entities.description: The immediate printed source named by the U-M XML. Independent critical-edition evidence describes Kinsley's whole poem as based primarily on the early print, with variants from that print and the Maitland Folio. Because the print lacks lines 1-103, another source was required for the opening, but the evidence inspected does not establish whether Kinsley transcribed the manuscript directly or followed an intermediary edition.

bibliographic.citation: William Dunbar, The Poems of William Dunbar, edited by James Kinsley, Oxford English Texts (Oxford and New York: Clarendon Press, Oxford University Press, 1979), pp. 42-58.
bibliographic.title: The Poems of William Dunbar

entities.label: Maitland Folio, Magdalene College Pepys MS 2553, pages 81-96
entities.description: A beginning-to-end manuscript witness of the poem, copied about 1570-1585/1586. Its rubric and attribution occur on page 81 and its explicit, 'Quod maister Williame Dunbar,' on page 96. It contains damaged or corrupt readings and omits three lines preserved by the print, including lines 360-361 in Salisbury's numbering, so it is not textually complete relative to the other witness.

date_statements.label: Witness copied
date_statements.notes: CELM gives c. 1570-85; other descriptions extend Maitland Folio production to 1586.

physical_description.extent: Poem from opening rubric to explicit on pages 81-96, with internal omissions relative to the early print
notes: This record describes the work-bearing portion, not a complete codicological description of the manuscript.

entities.label: The tua mariit wemen and the wedo, sole surviving early-print exemplar, NLS Sa.6 item 10
entities.description: An undated early printed tract conjecturally produced in Scotland around 1507, preserved as the sole surviving exemplar in NLS Sa.6. The exemplar lacks its first two leaves and transmits poem lines 104-530 on bound-volume images 177-189; image 189 contains lines 527-530 and then begins the following work. It ends with 'quod dunbar.' NLS identifies it as an additional item not printed by Chepman and Myllar.

date_statements.label: Printing
date_statements.value: [Scotland? : printer of The tua mariit wemen, 1507?]
date_statements.notes: Date, place, and printer remain conjectural; typographic difference and NLS collection guidance rule out automatic attribution to Chepman and Myllar.

bibliographic.citation: William Dunbar, [The tua mariit wemen and the wedo. And other poems], [Scotland? : printer of The tua mariit wemen, 1507?], STC 7350; sole surviving exemplar NLS Sa.6, item 10, imperfect.
bibliographic.title: [The tua mariit wemen and the wedo. And other poems]
bibliographic.pages: signatures a3-b3; bound-volume images 177-189 for this poem, with image 189 shared with the following work

holding.notes: Item/tract 10 in the bound volume commonly called the Chepman and Myllar Prints.

notes: The witness is physically incomplete: all before signature a3, corresponding to poem lines 1-103, is absent.

The schema has no early-printed-witness entity type; catalog_description is used for this copy-level printed object, following repository precedent, and the description and holding retain its physical-witness status.

entities.label: Complete 220-image published digital sequence of NLS Sa.6
entities.description: A complete downloadable 220-image published sequence of NLS Sa.6, uploaded to Wikimedia Commons from NLS page images. It reproduces printed leaves but not covers, boards, spine, or flyleaves and therefore is not a complete photographic record of the physical binding. The Dunbar poem begins at image 177 with line 104 and ends on image 189 with lines 527-530; the remainder of image 189 begins the next work.
entities.reproduction_format: 220-page DJVU assembled from NLS page images

entities.label: EEBO-TCP Phase I encoding A20974
entities.description: A diplomatic, lightly structured TEI/XML encoding created from EEBO images of the imperfect early-print tract. Its first div transcribes the surviving portion of this poem, lines 104-530; the complete XML encodes the whole surviving four-poem tract.

entities.label: Digital Index of Middle English Verse record 6134
entities.description: DIMEV v1.0 describes the poem as 530 unrhymed alliterative long lines, attributes it to William Dunbar, and records both the Maitland manuscript witness and the imperfect STC 7350 print.

entities.label: W. A. Craigie, The Maitland Folio Manuscript, volume 1 (1919), pages 98-115
entities.description: A scholarly transcription of the poem from the Maitland Folio manuscript. Its 1919 publication is public domain in the United States, but Craigie's original editorial contributions may remain protected in the United Kingdom through 2027; the NLS digitization layer is recorded separately under CC0.

bibliographic.citation: W. A. Craigie, ed., The Maitland Folio Manuscript, vol. 1, Scottish Text Society (Edinburgh and London, 1919), pp. 98-115.
bibliographic.title: The Maitland Folio Manuscript

entities.label: George Stevenson, Pieces from the Makculloch and Gray MSS. together with the Chepman and Myllar Prints (1918), pages 247-259
entities.description: A public-domain scholarly transcription of the surviving early-print text, and therefore only poem lines 104-530.

bibliographic.citation: George Stevenson, ed., Pieces from the Makculloch and Gray MSS. together with the Chepman and Myllar Prints, Scottish Text Society (Edinburgh and London, 1918), pp. 247-259.
bibliographic.title: Pieces from the Makculloch and Gray MSS. together with the Chepman and Myllar Prints

entities.label: David Laing, The Poems of William Dunbar, volume 1 (1834), pages 61-80
entities.description: An earlier public-domain edition of the poem, useful as a collateral source but not the printed source of the repository XML.

bibliographic.citation: David Laing, ed., The Poems of William Dunbar, now first collected, with notes, and a memoir of his life, vol. 1 (Edinburgh: Laing and Forbes, 1834), pp. 61-80.
bibliographic.title: The Poems of William Dunbar, now first collected, with notes, and a memoir of his life

entities.label: Internet Archive/Google scan of Laing's 1834 volume 1
entities.description: A complete 361-page scan visually verified as Laing's 1834 volume 1. Internet Archive's metadata for this item incorrectly identifies it as Kinsley's 1979 edition.

entities.label: Patricia Rickly, William Dunbar's Tretis: A Critical Edition (1980)
entities.description: A doctoral critical edition whose textual introduction documents both witnesses and earlier editions. Rickly supplies lines 1-103 from the Maitland Folio and uses the early print from line 104 onward while recording variants.

bibliographic.citation: Patricia Rickly, William Dunbar's 'Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo': A Critical Edition (PhD diss., University of Rhode Island, 1980).
bibliographic.title: William Dunbar's 'Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo': A Critical Edition

entities.label: Eve Salisbury, The Trials and Joys of Marriage (2002), The Tretis section
entities.description: A later teaching edition published by Medieval Institute Publications/TEAMS. The complete anthology is openly downloadable; the Dunbar section occupies PDF images 136-165, printed pages 135-164, with edited text on printed pages 135-151.

bibliographic.citation: Eve Salisbury, ed., The Trials and Joys of Marriage (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2002), pp. 135-164.
bibliographic.title: The Trials and Joys of Marriage

access.access_method: Read the checked-out CME submodule path; use the pinned GitHub blob for an immutable public reference.
Open the U-M item page; the stable name.umdl handle redirects to the same item.
Borrow the restricted Internet Archive item with an account; use the Folger record for public bibliographic verification.

local_copies.label: Internet Archive metadata for the restricted Kinsley item
notes: The public item files do not include a reusable full scan; lending derivatives are encrypted/restricted.

access.access_method: The official Pepys Digital Library is a selection and does not list MS 2553.

Contact the Pepys Librarian well in advance, identify Pepys MS 2553 and pages 81-96, and provide the requested research details. Appointments are currently offered Monday-Friday, 10:00-13:00 and 14:00-17:00, subject to advance arrangement and the library's limited restoration-period service.
access.format: Onsite consultation of the physical manuscript

access.access_method: Email the Pepys Library with shelfmark Pepys MS 2553, requested pages 81-96 or a request for the whole manuscript, intended use, and a request for a quote and publication terms.
access.format: Repository-supplied photography, terms and format by quotation
notes: The current researcher page directs requests for photographic services to the Pepys Librarian; service is limited during restoration, so confirm whether whole-manuscript photography is feasible.
The Pepys Digital Library says its published images must be purchased and publication permission obtained for reuse; request written terms for any newly supplied MS 2553 photographs.

access.access_method: Use the NLS viewer at image 177 for the work or download the complete published 220-image DJVU sequence from Wikimedia Commons.

notes: Image 177 begins 'He wil my corse...', poem line 104. Image 189 contains poem lines 527-530 and the explicit, then begins the following work; it is therefore a shared boundary image.

Coverage is complete for the published 220-image digital sequence, not for the physical bound object: inspection of images 1 and 220 found printed leaves rather than covers, boards, spine, or flyleaves.
This is a volume-level digital sequence, not a work-only crop.

rights.component: The medieval poem as an abstract linguistic work
rights.basis: The poem was composed and first attested in the early sixteenth century and its author died about 1520.
notes: This does not place modern editions, annotations, transcriptions, or photographs in the public domain.

rights.component: The materials presented through U-M CME item DunTwa
rights.basis: U-M identifies the item/collection as public domain and applies the No Copyright-United States rights statement while requesting educational and research use attribution.
notes: The U-M statement is jurisdiction-specific and should not be inherited by the 1979 printed edition as a whole.

rights.component: The checked-in XML copy of the U-M DunTwa encoding
rights.basis: The artifact is modeled as a copy of the U-M item for which U-M supplies a No Copyright-United States statement.
notes: The pinned CME repository contains an Unlicense text that repeatedly applies its dedication to 'software.' Whether that repository-level wording covers the XML text, data, or markup is ambiguous, so this rights conclusion remains grounded in U-M's item-specific No Copyright-United States statement rather than extending the Unlicense by inference.

rights.component: Kinsley's 1979 editorial text, apparatus, notes, selection, and typography
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1979 and no open license or item-specific public-domain determination for its modern editorial contributions was found.
notes: The medieval underlying text is public domain; the status here concerns potentially original modern editorial contributions.

rights.component: The sixteenth-century manuscript text of the poem
rights.basis: The manuscript witness was copied in the sixteenth century and the underlying text is medieval.

rights.component: New repository-supplied photography and permission to publish it
rights.basis: The Pepys Digital Library states that images must be purchased and permission obtained for publication; no quote or license terms for MS 2553 have yet been obtained.

rights.component: The early printed text and historical typography in the surviving exemplar
rights.basis: The tract was printed about 1507 and its underlying poem is medieval.

rights.component: The complete 220-image published digital sequence and faithful page images
rights.basis: Wikimedia Commons marks the file public domain/Public Domain Mark. NLS's current copyright guidance states that faithful digitizations have the status of the original and waives, under CC0, rights NLS may hold in its digitization layer.
notes: The legacy NLS microsite carries a 2006 blanket copyright notice, but NLS's updated 2026 guidance warns that older microsite statements may be outdated or incorrect. No modern item-specific NLS rights label was found.

rights.component: The EEBO-TCP Phase I XML transcription and markup
rights.basis: EEBO-TCP Phase I texts are released under CC0 and the file header identifies this item as a Phase I encoding.

rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data, including record 6134 and its witness descriptions
rights.basis: The Zenodo v1.0 release specifies Creative Commons Attribution 4.0.

rights.component: The 1919 edition, including Craigie's editorial contributions
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1919. Under the U.S. Copyright Office's duration guidance for works published before 1978, any maximum 95-year publication term expired before 2026.

rights.component: Craigie's potentially original editorial contributions to the 1919 edition
rights.basis: The British Academy records Craigie's death in 1957, and UK Intellectual Property Office guidance gives literary works a usual term of life plus 70 years. Original editorial contributions may therefore remain protected through 31 December 2027; no item-specific determination was found.
notes: This cautious statement does not apply to the sixteenth-century underlying poem or manuscript readings.

rights.component: Rights NLS may hold in its faithful digitization layer
rights.basis: NLS states that faithful digitizations retain the status of the original and waives under CC0 any rights it may hold in its digitization layer. This waiver is separate from the territorial status of Craigie's editorial contributions.

license.name: CC0 1.0 Universal for NLS digitization-layer rights

notes: No item-specific modern rights label was found for this PDF.

rights.component: The 1918 edition and NLS faithful digitization
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1918; NLS's current policy treats faithful digitizations according to the original and waives its digitization-layer rights under CC0.

license.name: CC0 1.0 Universal for NLS digitization-layer rights

notes: No item-specific modern rights label was found for this PDF.

rights.component: Laing's 1834 edition and the Internet Archive/Google scan
rights.basis: The underlying edition was published in 1834 and is public domain; the scan is publicly downloadable.
notes: The embedded Google notice requests noncommercial use and attribution. That provider notice is recorded separately from the public-domain copyright status of the underlying edition.

rights.component: Rickly's 1980 dissertation, critical edition, commentary, and apparatus
rights.basis: The repository record states all rights reserved and supplies no open license.
notes: Public download access is not an open publication license.

rights.component: Salisbury's 2002 edited text, introduction, notes, and downloadable edition PDF
rights.basis: The modern edition is openly readable and downloadable, but no CC or other open license for republication was found on the inspected work and introduction pages.
notes: Open access should not be conflated with permission to republish the modern editorial layer.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Kinsley's complete printed edition surrounding the poem, including editorial notes and front/back matter
editorial_practices.representation: The poem is encoded, while printed front matter, back matter, and editorial notes are omitted.
editorial_practices.description: The XML encoding declaration explicitly records these omissions.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A continuous poem arranged in speech and verse-paragraph groups
editorial_practices.representation: Six DIV1 containers and six range headings are explicitly marked as supplied line groups: 1-88, 89-173, 174-295, 296-384, 385-450, and 451-530.
editorial_practices.description: These supplied divisions support electronic navigation and must not be interpreted as authorial chapters or stanza boundaries.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A beginning-to-end but internally defective manuscript witness and an early-print witness missing lines 1-103
editorial_practices.representation: Kinsley presents the complete poem, bases the edition primarily on the print, and reports variants from the print and Maitland. The inspected evidence does not establish whether he took lines 1-103 directly from the manuscript or through an intermediary edition.
editorial_practices.description: Rickly's 1980 discussion supports the two-witness collation model but not a direct Kinsley-to-manuscript transcription path for the opening.
editorial_practices.scope: The complete poem; the immediate source route for lines 1-103 remains unresolved

editorial_practices.source_feature: Contractions in the Chepman and Myllar print
editorial_practices.representation: Rickly states that supplied expansion letters are italicized.
editorial_practices.description: This convention belongs to Rickly's later edition and cannot be projected onto Kinsley's typography or the U-M XML without direct comparison.
editorial_practices.scope: Rickly's transcription of the surviving print text

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_from_CTA/DunTwa.xml, pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: FILEDESC, SOURCEDESC, ENCODINGDESC, PROFILEDESC, PB milestones, DIV1/LG/L structure
evidence.summary: The XML names Kinsley's 1979 edition, pages 42-58, as source; dates the electronic text to 1993; records omission of front/back matter and editorial notes; and encodes 530 verse lines in 26 LG elements and six explicitly supplied navigation divisions.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, LICENSE, pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: The repository contains the Unlicense dedication, but the text repeatedly identifies its subject as 'software.' It does not expressly state whether XML text, data, or markup is included.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, 'William Dunbar's The tretis of the twa mariit women and the wedo,' item DunTwa.
evidence.summary: The live item confirms public full-text access, the DunTwa identifier, and U-M's public-domain/No Copyright-United States statement with an educational and research use request.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog, record 53769, The Poems of William Dunbar, edited by James Kinsley, 1979.
evidence.summary: The catalog verifies author, editor, title, publication places, publisher, date, series, and physical extent for Kinsley's edition.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for restricted item poemsofwilliamdu0000dunb_q8w2.
evidence.summary: The item metadata identifies Kinsley's 1979 Oxford/New York edition and its 508-page extent; the reusable public files are metadata while the scan derivatives are lending-restricted/encrypted.

evidence.citation: Patricia Rickly, William Dunbar's 'Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo': A Critical Edition (PhD diss., University of Rhode Island, 1980), doi:10.23860/diss-3044.
evidence.locator: abstract; introduction discussing Kinsley; textual introduction; editorial principles
evidence.summary: Rickly documents the sole print's missing first two leaves, Maitland's beginning-to-end but sometimes damaged text and three internal omissions relative to the print, her own use of Maitland for lines 1-103 and the print thereafter, and Kinsley's primarily print-based whole poem with variants from both witnesses. She does not establish whether Kinsley took his opening directly from the manuscript or through an intermediary. She also states that her expansions of print contractions appear in italics.

evidence.citation: Catalogue of English Literary Manuscripts, William Dunbar, DuW 185, and Magdalene College Pepys Library repository record.
evidence.summary: CELM records the poem in Magdalene College Pepys MS 2553, pages 81-96, with title and attribution on page 81, explicit on page 96, and manuscript date c. 1570-85. It also repeats an older Edinburgh 1508/Chepman-and-Myllar first-publication attribution that conflicts with current DIMEV/EEBO cataloging and NLS's separation of this tract from Chepman and Myllar's output.

evidence.citation: Magdalene College, Pepys Library.
evidence.summary: The official institutional page identifies the Pepys Library and provides the access context for its collections.

evidence.citation: Magdalene College, 'Pepys Library Information for Researchers.'
evidence.summary: The page gives the library's email and telephone contact, states that appointments are currently available Monday-Friday during specified hours by advance arrangement, explains that restoration limits service, links the reader guidelines, and directs enquiries about photographic services to the Pepys Librarian.

evidence.citation: Magdalene College, 'Pepys Digital Library.'
evidence.summary: The official page describes a selection of digitized Pepys manuscripts and does not list MS 2553. It also states that images must be purchased and permission obtained for publication.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, v1.0, record 6134, DOI 10.5281/zenodo.14585961.
evidence.locator: record 6134; IMEV 3845.5; linked manuscript and printed-book witness entries
evidence.summary: DIMEV attributes the 530 unrhymed alliterative long lines to William Dunbar; supplies the exact subjects 'women, against,' 'debates,' and 'courtly love, burlesque of'; and records the Pepys MS 2553 and imperfect STC 7350 witnesses with precise locators and edition references. The data release is CC BY 4.0.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, First Scottish Books, 'Items in the Chepman and Myllar Prints.'
evidence.summary: NLS places The Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo in the additional-items section and expressly distinguishes those items from works printed by Chepman and Myllar.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, First Scottish Books page-image viewer, NLS Sa.6, images 177-189.
evidence.summary: The official viewer presents the surviving early-print poem from image 177 through the top of image 189; image 177 begins at line 104 because two leaves are missing, while image 189 contains lines 527-530 and the explicit before the following work begins on the same image.

evidence.citation: Wikimedia Commons, File:Chepman and Myllar.djvu, sourced from National Library of Scotland images.
evidence.locator: 220-image file; work on images 177-189, with image 189 shared
evidence.summary: The file description identifies NLS as source, provides the complete published 220-image DJVU sequence, and marks the file public domain/Public Domain Mark. 'Complete' describes the digital sequence, not physical coverage of covers, boards, spine, or flyleaves.

evidence.citation: Local inspection of source-cache/DunTwa/chepman-and-myllar-prints-complete-220-pages.djvu.
evidence.summary: The DJVU reports 220 images. Image 177 visibly begins 'He wil my corse...', corresponding to poem line 104. Image 188 ends at line 526; image 189 contains lines 527-530 and 'Q Dunbar,' then begins the following work, making image 189 a shared boundary. Images 1 and 220 are printed leaves rather than covers, boards, spine, or flyleaves.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, 'Copyright,' updated 17 February 2026.
evidence.summary: NLS states that faithful digitizations have the copyright status of the original, waives under CC0 any rights it may hold in digitization layers, permits commercial reuse where a Public Domain Mark applies, and warns that older microsites may carry outdated or incorrect blanket copyright statements.

evidence.citation: EEBO-TCP Phase I, A20974, [The tua mariit wemen and the wedo. And other poems], XML encoding created 2005-12.
evidence.locator: TEI header and first body div
evidence.summary: The header identifies STC 7350, ESTC S114713, the NLS original, eleven source TIFFs, an imperfect exemplar lacking all before a3, conjectural Scotland/1507 publication, diplomatic/light structural encoding, and release of the Phase I text into the public domain; the current Oxford repository landing supplies the CC0 label. The first div transcribes poem lines 104-530.

evidence.citation: W. A. Craigie, ed., The Maitland Folio Manuscript, vol. 1 (Scottish Text Society, 1919), pp. 98-115, NLS digital copy.
evidence.summary: The complete 498-page published sequence transcribes the poem from the Maitland Folio at pages 98-115; its title page names Craigie as editor and states 'All Rights Reserved.'

evidence.citation: The British Academy, 'Craigie, William, 1867-1957.'
evidence.summary: The British Academy authority page identifies editor William Alexander Craigie as living 1867-1957.

evidence.citation: UK Intellectual Property Office, 'Copyright Notice: Duration of copyright (term),' published 15 January 2021.
evidence.locator: Rules by type of work or performance
evidence.summary: The official guidance gives literary works a usual term extending through 70 years after the author's death and cautions that exceptions can apply.

evidence.citation: United States Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright, revised April 2026.
evidence.summary: The official guidance describes the maximum 95-year term applicable to qualifying works first published before 1978; a work published in 1919 had completed that term before 2026.

evidence.citation: George Stevenson, ed., Pieces from the Makculloch and Gray MSS. together with the Chepman and Myllar Prints (Scottish Text Society, 1918), pp. 247-259, NLS digital copy.
evidence.summary: The complete 360-page volume transcribes the surviving early-print portion of the poem at pages 247-259.

evidence.citation: David Laing, ed., The Poems of William Dunbar, vol. 1 (Edinburgh: Laing and Forbes, 1834), pp. 61-80, Internet Archive/Google scan.
evidence.locator: scanned title page, contents, and printed pages 61-80
evidence.summary: Visual inspection identifies the complete 361-page PDF as Laing's 1834 volume 1 and locates the poem on printed pages 61-80.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive item poemswilliamdun02dunbgoog: item metadata compared with the downloaded PDF's title page and contents.
evidence.summary: The provider metadata calls the item Kinsley's 1979 Poems of William Dunbar, but the actual 361-page scan is unambiguously David Laing's 1834 volume 1. The mismatch prevents use of this public scan as evidence of Kinsley's text.

evidence.citation: Eve Salisbury, ed., The Trials and Joys of Marriage (Medieval Institute Publications, 2002), METS edition and complete downloadable PDF.
evidence.locator: PDF images 136-165; printed pages 135-164; edited text pages 135-151; online introduction
evidence.summary: The complete 277-page anthology includes Salisbury's edited poem, notes, and introduction. The edition cites both MS and CM readings; its framing supports classification as social satire with fabliau elements, a female drinking community, and marriage debate.

open_questions.question: What immediate source did Kinsley use for poem lines 1-103, which are absent from the surviving early print?
next_steps: Inspect Kinsley pages 42-58 and the edition's textual introduction or apparatus for an explicit source statement for lines 1-103.
Determine whether Kinsley transcribed Pepys MS 2553 directly or followed an intermediary printed edition before adding any direct manuscript transcription edge.
notes: Rickly establishes that Kinsley presents the whole poem, bases his edition primarily on the print, and records variants from print and Maitland; this does not by itself identify the immediate source of his opening.

open_questions.question: What exact typographic and editorial distinctions in Kinsley pages 42-58 were flattened, omitted, or structurally transformed in the U-M XML?
next_steps: Borrow or inspect an authorized copy of Kinsley's 1979 edition and compare pages 42-58 character by character with the XML.
Pay particular attention to italic letters, abbreviation expansions, punctuation, capitalization, apparatus, and paragraph/speech divisions.
notes: Rickly's explicit use of italics for her own expansion letters does not establish Kinsley's convention.

open_questions.question: Can Magdalene College supply a complete high-resolution facsimile of Pepys MS 2553, or at minimum pages 81-96, and what reuse terms and fee would apply?
next_steps: Email pepyslibrary@magd.cam.ac.uk with the shelfmark, work pages, request for whole-manuscript coverage if feasible, desired resolution/color, and intended scholarly/reference use.
Request a written quote and separate permission terms for private research, repository reference, and any publication.

open_questions.question: Where, when, and by whom was STC 7350 actually printed?
next_steps: Consult the latest ESTC record and specialist typographical scholarship to test the traditional 'Rouen print' attribution; the currently attached DIMEV, EEBO-TCP, and NLS evidence supports only the cautious '[Scotland?]' formulation.
Preserve the current '[Scotland? : printer of The tua mariit wemen, 1507?]' formulation unless firmer evidence emerges.

open_questions.question: Will NLS provide a modern item-specific rights statement for the Sa.6 facsimile that reconciles the legacy microsite notice with its current CC0/public-domain guidance?
next_steps: Ask NLS rights staff to confirm reuse terms for the exact Sa.6 images if a publication requires institution-specific written assurance.
Retain the Commons Public Domain Mark and the current general NLS policy as separate evidence.
